title: Untitled
research_problem: The paper investigates pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities (signalized/unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, physical barriers, shelters, colored paving) in a divided urban boulevard next to Michigan State University.
method_summary: Mixed-methods study combining: (1) video-camera observation of pedestrian movements using 8 cameras at study site over multiple sessions during peak periods, and (2) user survey administered electronically to 711 pedestrians (daily and occasional users) covering crossing patterns, preferences, and perceptions.
method_family: observational_study_survey_research
tasks: pedestrian_crossing_compliance_measurement; pedestrian_perception_assessment; factor_analysis_of_crossing_choices; comparison_of_survey_and_observed_behavior
datasets: Grand River Avenue (M-43), East Lansing MI - 1km divided boulevard section from Abbott to Bogue Streets; Survey data: 711 completed questionnaires (from 897 received, after removing 186 non-user or incomplete responses); Video data: 16-18 hours of recorded pedestrian movements across study site
metrics: Spatial_Crossing_Compliance_Rate_SCCR; Temporal_Crossing_Compliance_Rate_TCCR; crossing_location_preferences_percent; perceived_safety_rating; factor_influence_ratings
claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) with high crossing compliance rate (71.2%)
limitations: Study conducted at single location (university campus corridor) - limited geographic generalizability | Survey response rate was 17.9% - potential for non-response bias | Sample predominantly university affiliates (students, staff, faculty) - may not represent general population | Data collection limited to Michigan State University session periods (Spring/Fall 1998), excluding weekends and adverse weather | Self-reported survey data may be subject to social desirability bias
notes: Study site was renovated Grand River Avenue corridor with multiple facility types in same environment. SCCR averages 71.4% across all crosswalk types. Signalized crosswalks showed highest SCCR (82.8%) but lower temporal compliance. Survey responses showed good consistency with observed behavior (59% spatial compliance from survey vs 71.4% observed).

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a pedestrian micro-simulation model for Chinese cities that accounts for the high rate of red light running and mixed traffic characteristics, which differ significantly from developed countries
method_summary: A pedestrian micro-simulation model classifying pedestrians into two types (law-obeying and opportunistic) with parameters calibrated via questionnaires (n=495) and time gap distributions extracted from videotape (989 samples). The model incorporates external factors including policeman presence, vehicle flow, and other pedestrians' behavior to determine crossing decisions during red light
method_family: Agent-based micro-simulation with behavioral modeling
tasks: Pedestrian road-crossing behavior simulation; Red light running prediction; Mixed traffic flow modeling
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing at Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples); Field observation data from crossings without police (June 2003 - April 2004)
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Pedestrian arriving interval distribution; Time gap acceptance (ΔT)
claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with adjusted simulation results closely matching field observations (mean red light runner proportion: 0.82 simulated vs 0.85 field)
limitations: The model only considers pedestrians' decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicle state changes (acceleration/deceleration) | The calculation method of time gap (ΔT) needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | The model simplifies vehicle behavior by treating turning vehicles as straight-going vehicles | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians, and answers may not reflect actual daily behavior, leading to calibration uncertainties
notes: The model addresses a critical gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries, particularly China where pedestrian violation rates can reach 66.7% in some sections. The approach of using both questionnaires and videotape for calibration is novel, though the paper acknowledges the deviation between simulated and field results before parameter adjustment.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Limited pedestrian behavior models exist for signalized crosswalks, where pedestrian behavior involves group or individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, response to signal control, and crosswalk boundary. The study addresses this gap by developing a microscopic simulation model for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized intersections.
method_summary: A modified social force model is developed based on pedestrian psychological process, incorporating five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (both group evasive and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. The model includes desired direction model using Weibull distribution, stop/go decision model using binary Logit for pedestrian flashing green phase, and collision avoidance mechanisms. Parameters are separated into measurable (directly estimated from trajectory data) and non-measurable (derived by maximum likelihood estimation) groups.
method_family: Microscopic pedestrian simulation/Social force model/Force-based model
tasks: Pedestrian trajectory estimation; Pedestrian crossing behavior simulation at signalized crosswalk; Collision avoidance modeling with vehicles; Lane-formation phenomenon reproduction; Stop/go decision modeling at PFG phase
datasets: Video data from Kanayama intersection, Nagoya City, Japan - 1352 pedestrians and 124 left-turning vehicles; 97,200 position samples for trajectory profiles
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation: 10.5%; Two-sample t-test for passing position distribution at cross-sections; Lane formation count comparison; Instant angle distribution between current and desired direction; Passing time distribution at crosswalk
claim_text: The desired direction model using Weibull distribution enables realistic representation of pedestrian maneuver toward predetermined positions at crosswalk, incorporating crosswalk geometry, pedestrian origin-destination movement, and density factors.
limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world - the repulsive force from turning vehicle needs improvement | Pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship are not considered as influencing factors | The visual range parameters (angle 170 degrees, radius 8m) are adopted from related studies rather than directly estimated from the trajectory dataset due to data collection limitations
notes: Study funded by Committee on Advanced Road Technology (CART) of Road Bureau, Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT), Japan. Data collected from north crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City using video image processing software.

datasets: Video data from Kanayama intersection, Nagoya City, Japan - 1352 pedestrians and 124 left-turning vehicles; 97,200 position samples for trajectory profiles
metrics: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position estimation: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed estimation: 10.5%; Two-sample t-test for passing position distribution at cross-sections; Lane formation count comparison; Instant angle distribution between current and desired direction; Passing time distribution at crosswalk
claim_text: The coefficient for vehicle repulsive force interaction strength has the largest parameter value, indicating highest sensitivity of pedestrian behavior to collision avoidance with vehicles.
limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world - the repulsive force from turning vehicle needs improvement | Pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship are not considered as influencing factors | The visual range parameters (angle 170 degrees, radius 8m) are adopted from related studies rather than directly estimated from the trajectory dataset due to data collection limitations
notes: Study funded by Committee on Advanced Road Technology (CART) of Road Bureau, Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT), Japan. Data collected from north crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City using video image processing software.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a pedestrian microsimulation model for Chinese cities that accurately represents the high rate of signal non-compliance (red light running) and mixed traffic characteristics, as existing foreign micro-simulation tools (CORSIM, SIMTRAFFIC) fail to capture Chinese traffic conditions due to different behavioral patterns
method_summary: A pedestrian microsimulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types (law-obeying Type I and opportunistic Type II), uses questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions and external factor influences, and extracts time gap distribution (DT) from videotape data (989 samples) to calibrate the critical gap acceptance criterion for pedestrian crossing decisions
method_family: microsimulation_agent_based_modeling
tasks: pedestrian_road_crossing_behavior_simulation; modeling_red_light_violation; gap_acceptance_modeling
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples)
metrics: proportion_of_red_light_jumpers; proportion_of_violation_followers; distribution_of_pedestrian_arriving_intervals
claim_text: The developed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running (45-83% violation rate) and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
limitations: The model only considers pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle state changes (acceleration/deceleration) during crossing | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrian populations; self-reported behavior may differ from actual behavior | The DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | Pedestrians' estimation of vehicle velocity cannot be directly extracted from videotape, requiring use of instantaneous speed as approximation
notes: Model parameters calibrated: r1=0.55 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians), r2 adjusted from 0.69 to 0.35 based on validation; r3 derived from videotape gap acceptance data. Pedestrian average walking speed used: 1.2 m/s. Key finding: presence of other violating pedestrians has strong influence on signal non-compliance (30% report high/very high probability), while policeman presence has surprisingly low impact (only 2% high/very high).

title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intentions at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
method_summary: VR experiment with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Used a 4x4 randomized complete block design testing 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, control). Collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time from headset tracking) and subjective measures (feature ratings, receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment, Randomized Complete Block Design
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision at crosswalk; Pedestrian-FAV interaction evaluation
datasets: Virtual environment developed using Unity 3D with HTC Vive VR headset
metrics: Feature ratings (1-7 Likert scale); Crossing time (seconds); Waiting time before crossing; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF); Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) - 20-item short version; Personal Innovativeness scale; Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
claim_text: Inclusion of external features significantly increased pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs
limitations: Study used a non-signalized intersection with single controlled scenario where FAV approached from one direction and stopped at intersection | No other traffic was included in the virtual environment to eliminate distractions, reducing ecological validity | Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial - did not study feature termination or negative signals | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability of findings | Participants were primarily from US and all fluent English speakers; findings may not generalize to other cultures or language groups
notes: Validated VR platform for pedestrian safety research (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017). First-time exposure for all participants to FAVs. Walking silhouette considered familiar as it mimics existing pedestrian crossing signals. Verbal message beneficial for visually impaired pedestrians and those distracted. Smiley face confused participants about vehicle's intended action.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying and extending the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) with new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency.
method_summary: Survey-based study using structured questionnaire administered in Dalian, China. Data analyzed with Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) using maximum likelihood estimation. Four models were tested: Model 1 (basic TPB: instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control), Model 2 (adding descriptive norm), Model 3 (adding perceived risk), and Model 4 (adding conformity tendency).
method_family: Quantitative, Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), Survey research
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intention (crossing against pedestrian traffic lights)
datasets: 260 complete and valid questionnaire responses from pedestrians in Dalian, China
metrics: CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOSE; CFI (Comparative Fit Index); Cronbach's alpha for reliability; Regression weights (unstandardized)
claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention in all four models (p<0.001 in all cases)
limitations: Self-reported behavior may be biased, especially for negative behaviors | Relatively small number of items in each construct to maintain high internal reliability | Perceived behavioral control was not significant, contrary to TPB theory expectations - may need more risky scenarios to demonstrate effect
notes: Study was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 respondents (57.3% female, 42.7% male); Model 4 with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency explained pedestrian violating crossing intentions better than basic TPB; Findings suggest pedestrians' observations of family/friends' behavior have greater influence than perceived approval from them; Results support using descriptive norms and conformity tendency in safety interventions to reduce pedestrian violations

title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior while Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker captured visual attention data. Participants completed 8 crossing trials under 4 conditions: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Three crossing behavior variables and five eye movement variables were measured.
method_family: Controlled field experiment
tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection
datasets: Not applicable - experimental study rather than dataset analysis
metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Look left and right (frequency); Crossing speed (m/s); Pupil diameter (mm); Scanning frequency (scans per unit time); Fixation points (count per AOI); Fixation time (percentage per AOI); Fixation duration (seconds per AOI)
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
limitations: Sample limited to college students (age 17-25), limiting generalizability to broader pedestrian population | Small sample size (n=28) may limit statistical power and generalizability | Experiment conducted at one signalized intersection with low traffic volume (fewer than 5 vehicles per minute), may not generalize to higher traffic or non-signalized intersections | Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone (Huawei P9) rather than their own, may not reflect real-world behavior | Mental arithmetic tasks used for phone conversation and text conditions may not accurately represent typical phone use patterns | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment
notes: Study addresses growing pedestrian safety concern with mobile phone distraction. Three distraction types compared: music (audio only), phone conversation (cognitive + audio), and text (cognitive + visual + manual). Findings suggest text distraction poses the greatest risk due to combined cognitive, visual, and manual demands. Research has implications for pedestrian safety policy and infrastructure design.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running violation propensity at signalized intersections in urban Hong Kong, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal periods across 652 cycles. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity.
method_family: discrete_choice_model_random_parameter_logit
tasks: analyze_pedestrian_red_light_violation_decision; identify_personal_factors_affecting_violation; identify_environmental_factors_affecting_violation; examine_interaction_effects_between_factors; measure_social_influence_of_other_pedestrians
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; 6 locations: Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
metrics: binary_logit_coefficients; odds_ratios; Akaike_Information_Criterion_AIC; elasticity_measurements; statistical_significance_z_values
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower red light running propensity compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649)
limitations: Study focuses on factors affecting red light running propensity but does not assess safety outcomes or associated crash risk | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior was not measured despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Information on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and safety awareness not available in observation survey; only behavioral data collected | Sample limited to urban crosswalks in Hong Kong with specific characteristics (high pedestrian/vehicle volumes, no countdown signals); generalizability to other contexts may be limited
notes: Study uniquely combines individual-level (pedestrian demographics, behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors in same model. Novel examination of social norm effects through presence/behavior of other pedestrians. Model 2 with interaction terms shows superior fit (AIC=2750.1 vs 2776.6). Random parameter approach justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect (1.163).

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior and modeling pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections during the red-man phase of traffic light signals
method_summary: The paper proposes a new statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections. It starts from a bounded Pareto distribution for a specific pedestrian under given traffic conditions, then uses a generalized linear model to relate waiting time to vehicle time headway, and finally employs statistical unconditioning to derive the unconditional distribution accounting for vehicle-pedestrian interaction. This leads to a mixture distribution with four components that exhibits a U-shaped pattern.
method_family: mixture_distribution_model
tasks: modeling_pedestrian_waiting_times; pedestrian_behavior_analysis
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations, collected on two consecutive weekend days)
metrics: chi-square_goodness_of_fit_test; median; interquartile_range
claim_text: Pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections follow a U-shaped distribution, characterized by a large proportion crossing immediately and a large proportion waiting for the entire red-man phase
limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only waiting time as the explicit variable, limiting ability to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors | The paper does not address how the model performs at different types of intersections or under varying traffic control schemes beyond the single case study | Parameter estimation relies on EM algorithm which may face identifiability issues common in mixture models, particularly when some components converge to degenerate distributions
notes: The red-man phase duration in the study was 75 seconds with total signal cycle of 110 seconds; Key model parameters include effective critical headway (ECH) and sensitivity coefficient for excess headway; Risk-taking pedestrians have lower ECH while risk-averse pedestrians have higher ECH; Goodness-of-fit test (chi-square = 3.51) confirmed model validity at 5% significance level; Model was applied to analyze real field data from Kunming, China

title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections in China is a critical factor contributing to pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The study aims to explore how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples collected from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
method_family: binomial_logistic_regression
tasks: identify_significant_factors_affecting_red_light_running; predict_probability_of_red_light_running_behavior
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections, May-June 2015)
metrics: odds_ratio; statistical_significance_p_values; score_test
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period in a day (daytime vs dawn), attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), so results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only considered signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facilities like midblock crossings | Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for full generalization; more data from multiple intersections needed | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/herd mentality effects
notes: Study uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Four significant variables emerged from initial 15 explanatory variables; Model provides probability prediction function (Equation 3); Key policy implications: strengthen law enforcement in CBD areas, install electronic facilities, improve road crossing facilities and traffic signal control systems

The study aims to explore how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples collected from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
method_family: binomial_logistic_regression
tasks: identify_significant_factors_affecting_red_light_running; predict_probability_of_red_light_running_behavior
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections, May-June 2015)
metrics: odds_ratio; statistical_significance_p_values; score_test
claim_text: Strolling pedestrians are safer (lower red-light running probability) compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio 0.394)
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), so results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only considered signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facilities like midblock crossings | Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for full generalization; more data from multiple intersections needed | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/herd mentality effects
notes: Study uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Four significant variables emerged from initial 15 explanatory variables; Model provides probability prediction function (Equation 3); Key policy implications: strengthen law enforcement in CBD areas, install electronic facilities, improve road crossing facilities and traffic signal control systems

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify factors affecting the propensity of red light running violations of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic condition, presence of other pedestrians)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red time of 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure the association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation analysis; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior; Interaction effect analysis between personal and environmental factors
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks; 652 signal cycles in Hong Kong urban area; Data collected during peak and non-peak daytime periods; Sites were pedestrian crash hot spots (each with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: Binary outcome (red light running vs. not running); Odds ratios; Marginal effects/elasticities; AIC for model comparison (Model 1: 2776.6, Model 2: 2750.1); Statistical significance at 1%, 5%, and 10% levels
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
limitations: Safety outcome (associated crash risk) is out of study scope - only violation behavior analyzed, not actual crash outcomes | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not assessed | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and socioeconomic characteristics (education, income) were not collected - limits understanding of psychological factors | Study conducted in Hong Kong only - findings may not generalize to other contexts with different traffic regulations, cultural norms, or infrastructure
notes: First study to simultaneously examine individual-level (personal demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition and signal time) factors on red light running violation; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence of companion, number of pedestrians around, and violation behavior of others; Random parameter approach justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect (0.372 in Model 1, 1.163 in Model 2), indicating unobserved heterogeneity among female pedestrians; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1169 out of 6320) committed red light running violations; Hong Kong pedestrian signal system has three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red - no countdown display exists

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding and modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, and analyzing their effects on traffic flow.
method_summary: A microscopic traffic flow model was developed by integrating probabilistic models of vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance into a car-following model (Full Velocity Difference model). The model uses time-step simulation in MATLAB to describe the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians at unsignalized midblock crosswalks. Validation was performed using empirical data from two crosswalks in Shanghai.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model, Car-following model, Simulation-based analysis
tasks: Vehicle-pedestrian interaction modeling; Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors; Recommendations for control mode selection (unsignalized vs signalized)
datasets: Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h); Empirical data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h); VY and PGA models developed from data collected at 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai
metrics: Average vehicular delay (s); Average pedestrian waiting time (s); Relative error between model and survey results; Wilcoxon signed-rank test for model validation
claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, with maximum relative error less than 10% compared to empirical data
limitations: The study only considers legal pedestrian crossing behavior at designated crosswalks; illegal pedestrian crossing is not modeled | The model assumes simplified vehicle and pedestrian arrival distributions (Poisson) without considering effects from adjacent intersections | The accuracy depends on the separate VY and PGA behavioral models; combined model accuracy was not guaranteed
notes: The study uses a logit-based framework for both vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance decisions. The VY model considers gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median presence, and e-police presence. The PGA model considers gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. Key finding: unsignalized crosswalks with more than 4 lanes should be signalized or include central islands.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions in highly populous cities, addressing issues of crossing speed variations, signal compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions to improve pedestrian safety and infrastructure design
method_summary: Video-based field observations at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, India. Data collected from 775 pedestrian samples during peak hours. Statistical analyses performed using Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA test, and Student t test at 95% confidence interval. Logistic regression models developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and pedestrian-vehicular interactions using SPSS 16.0 software.
method_family: Empirical observational study with statistical modeling (logistic regression)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations; Identify factors affecting pedestrian crossing speed; Determine factors affecting pedestrian signal compliance; Identify factors influencing pedestrian-vehicular interaction; Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai: Swami Vivekanand Junction (166 samples), Mahim Junction (272 samples), Holkar Junction (337 samples)
metrics: Crossing speed (mean, standard deviation, 15th/50th/85th percentiles); Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF); Pedestrian compliance rate (green phase usage); Pedestrian-vehicular interaction rate; Odds Ratio (OR) from logistic regression
claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with design crossing speeds of 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians
limitations: Study conducted at only 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai, which may not represent all mixed traffic conditions | Sample size of 775 pedestrians, though substantial, may not capture all behavioral variations; authors suggest larger samples needed for better statistical results | The constant IRC value of 1.2 m/s used as baseline may not reflect current pedestrian behavior patterns
notes: Introduced Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF) as new metric to understand pedestrian speed variations; Logistic regression models developed for both noncompliance (n=434) and interaction (n=141) behaviors; Study found 56% overall pedestrian noncompliance rate at studied intersections; Mixed traffic conditions specific to Indian context ( Mumbai) with vehicles like auto/vans, taxis, two-wheelers and HCVs

title: Untitled
research_problem: Developing a probabilistic model to predict pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections for collision avoidance in connected and autonomous vehicles, specifically addressing the hazardous scenario of pedestrian-vehicle collisions at turning locations.
method_summary: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) that models relationships among traffic signal phases, pedestrian crossing decisions, motion types (standing/walking/running), and physical movement. The model uses particle filtering for Bayesian inference to estimate pedestrian states including position, crossing decision, and motion type from noisy position observations and signal phase information.
method_family: Probabilistic Graphical Models, Dynamic Bayesian Networks, Particle Filter
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait); Motion type classification (standing, walking, running); Position estimation from noisy observations
datasets: Real traffic data collected from a vehicle at a signalized intersection in Japan using three monocular cameras. Contains 289 pedestrian sequences: 251 cross-scenarios and 38 wait-scenarios. Pedestrian data includes position trajectories and motion types labeled manually.
metrics: Confusion matrices for decision recognition; Precision values for decision and motion recognition; Positioning error (mean and standard deviation in meters)
claim_text: The DBN+PF system can recognize crossing decisions within 2 seconds of observation and waiting decisions within 3-4 seconds across all noise levels with >85% accuracy
limitations: Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking motion due to overlapping speed distributions in the model (recognition rate ~35-56%) | Larger observation errors cause recognition delay and decrease accuracy - wait decision recognition takes 3-4s compared to 2s for cross decisions | The model assumes straight-line pedestrian trajectories, making position estimation less accurate for curved trajectories (common in cross-scenarios) | The model assumes pedestrians make crossing decisions primarily at the onset of PFG, but pedestrians may change decisions based on traffic situations (e.g., turning vehicles) | The system cannot predict paths for curved trajectories, limiting its application to path prediction tasks
notes: The paper focuses on near-side pedestrians at left-turning scenarios (Japan uses left-hand traffic). The three-phase pedestrian signal in Japan (PG/PFG/PR) is used as contextual information. The model assumes signal phase information can be obtained via V2I communication in connected vehicle settings.

metrics: Confusion matrices for decision recognition; Precision values for decision and motion recognition; Positioning error (mean and standard deviation in meters)
claim_text: The proposed method achieves >86% recognition accuracy for both cross and wait decisions under different measurement noise levels (rn = 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 m)
limitations: Running motion is difficult to distinguish from walking motion due to overlapping speed distributions in the model (recognition rate ~35-56%) | Larger observation errors cause recognition delay and decrease accuracy - wait decision recognition takes 3-4s compared to 2s for cross decisions | The model assumes straight-line pedestrian trajectories, making position estimation less accurate for curved trajectories (common in cross-scenarios) | The model assumes pedestrians make crossing decisions primarily at the onset of PFG, but pedestrians may change decisions based on traffic situations (e.g., turning vehicles) | The system cannot predict paths for curved trajectories, limiting its application to path prediction tasks
notes: The paper focuses on near-side pedestrians at left-turning scenarios (Japan uses left-hand traffic). The three-phase pedestrian signal in Japan (PG/PFG/PR) is used as contextual information. The model assumes signal phase information can be obtained via V2I communication in connected vehicle settings.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian safety at signalized intersections, specifically the challenge of modeling sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that pedestrians exhibit while crossing, which can lead to hazardous situations when drivers cannot anticipate these behavioral changes.
method_summary: The study develops a probabilistic discrete choice model to predict when pedestrians will change speed while crossing signalized crosswalks. The model uses stepwise speed functions to represent pedestrian speed profiles. It consists of two sub-models: (1) a speed change event choice model determining acceleration/deceleration probability based on necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec), current speed, conflict area presence, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase; (2) acceleration/deceleration magnitude sub-models using linear regression for acceleration and normal distribution for deceleration. Monte Carlo simulation is applied to generate complete pedestrian speed profiles.
method_family: Discrete choice model (multinomial logit), Regression analysis, Monte Carlo simulation
tasks: Model pedestrian speed change events (acceleration/deceleration); Predict timing and location of speed changes; Predict magnitude of speed changes; Safety assessment of pedestrian-vehicle conflicts using Post Encroachment Time (PET)
datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya, Japan (Kanayama, Ueda, Fushimi); Manual tracking using TrafficAnalyzer software at 0.5s intervals; 4009 aggregated data samples (5s intervals); Pedestrian demand: low-to-medium to minimize pedestrian-pedestrian interaction
metrics: Modified ρ² = 0.431 for speed change event choice model; Modified R² = 0.765 for acceleration sub-model; Modified R² = 0.725 for deceleration sub-model; Crossing time distribution comparison; Vehicle yield rate; Post Encroachment Time (PET)
claim_text: The proposed model can represent pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
limitations: Sample size limitation - data collected from only five sites, which may have prevented some variables (PG/PFG length, crosswalk length, elapsed time from PFG onset) from showing significance | The model tends to underestimate the probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in predicting actual acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction not considered in the model due to low traffic volumes at study sites | Pedestrian characteristics such as age, gender, and distraction (e.g., smartphone use) were not considered despite their potential impact on behavior | The model does not capture real-time interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles, only uses conflict area dummy variable
notes: The study was conducted in Japan where pedestrian signals do not include countdown timers. The model was integrated with vehicle maneuver models to assess safety using PET (Post Encroachment Time). Key application areas include traffic simulation for safety assessment, real-time warning systems for vehicles, and development of avoidance maneuver modeling for autonomous vehicles.
